teamObjective.explanation: Player 0 has only 2 cards left while every other player has 3, so the immediate team priority is to shed the remaining cards and secure first-out advantage.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner has not acted, so their exact hand is unknown. With 3 cards they may hold high cards, trumps, or jokers, and are likely trying to finish their own hand or protect our lead if possible.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents also hold 3 cards each and have revealed nothing. They may possess trumps, jokers, or high singles, and will likely try to beat any cheap lead to stop us from going out.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 2 is the strongest non-trump single in hand. It minimizes the set of cards opponents can use to win the trick (only trumps/jokers beat it), gives the highest chance of retaining the lead, and lets me play the A on the next lead to finish.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The A is lower than the 2, so more opponent cards can beat it. If the A loses, I also lose tempo and may never get to lead the 2. Even if the A wins, I still must play the 2 next and it could be beaten by a trump/joker, so ordering A first is strictly weaker.

risks: An opponent may have a trump or joker to beat the 2 and seize the lead.
After losing the lead, opponents might play combinations I cannot match, leaving me stuck on the A.
Partner may not have a high enough card to recapture the lead for me.
riskAssessment.mitigation: Leading the 2 maximizes my chance of winning the current trick and keeping initiative. If I lose it, I still hold an A, a relatively strong single that can beat many later leads.
